title: Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (PALS): Leading the Code on a Child
subtitle: A 3-year-old in severe respiratory distress deteriorates to bradycardia and poor perfusion, then arrest. The nurse must lead the pediatric code — prioritizing oxygenation and ventilation, applying PALS, and coordinating the team with weight-based dosing.

patient.name: Mateo Rivas
patient.admitDate: Pediatric unit for severe pneumonia and respiratory distress. Despite oxygen, he becomes lethargic and mottled with bradycardia and poor perfusion, then apneic and pulseless. Pediatric code team activated; nurse leading the resuscitation.

vitals.temp: HR <60 with poor perfusion — start CPR

nursesNote: Mateo Rivas, 3, with severe pneumonia and respiratory distress decompensating to bradycardia (HR 50) with poor perfusion, then apnea and pulselessness — a respiratory/hypoxic arrest, the typical pediatric pathway. Pediatric code activated. Priorities: prioritize airway, oxygenation, and ventilation; start CPR for HR <60 with poor perfusion (depth ~1/3 chest, 15:2 with two rescuers, rotate q2min); give weight-based medications using the length-based (Broselow) tape; defibrillate VF/pVT at 2 then 4 J/kg; treat reversible causes (hypoxia first). Lead with one designated leader, clear role assignment, and closed-loop communication.
providerOrders: Activate the pediatric code team; designate one leader and assign roles
Prioritize airway, oxygenation, and bag-mask ventilation
Start CPR if HR <60 with poor perfusion; depth ~1/3 chest, 15:2 with two rescuers
Weight-based medications via length-based (Broselow) tape; epinephrine per PALS
Defibrillate VF/pVT at 2 J/kg then 4 J/kg; treat reversible causes; transfer to PICU

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is happening in this pediatric arrest?

opts.text: Pediatric arrests are most often respiratory/hypoxic in origin
Recognizing and treating respiratory failure early can prevent arrest
Bradycardia with poor perfusion in a child is an ominous pre-arrest sign
A heart rate below 60 with poor perfusion despite oxygenation calls for CPR
Children almost always arrest from a primary cardiac rhythm like adults
Call for help and activate the pediatric code team early
High-quality CPR uses a depth of about one-third the chest's depth
With two rescuers, use a 15:2 compression-to-ventilation ratio
Effective oxygenation and ventilation are central in pediatric arrest
Use a length-based (Broselow) tape for weight-based drugs and equipment
Pediatric medication doses are the same fixed doses used for adults
One clear leader assigns roles and uses closed-loop communication

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the management of the code

opts.text: Recognize the deterioration; call for help and the pediatric code team
Open the airway and provide effective oxygenation and ventilation
Start high-quality CPR if HR <60 with poor perfusion
Give weight-based epinephrine (via the tape) and treat reversible causes
Reassess rhythm/perfusion, rotate compressors, and document
Start compressions if HR <60 with poor perfusion despite oxygenation
Use a 15:2 compression-to-ventilation ratio with two rescuers
Compress about one-third the depth of the chest
Use a length-based (Broselow) tape for weight-based doses
Prioritize effective oxygenation and ventilation
Rotate compressors about every 2 minutes
Delay CPR until the heart rate falls below 40
Give adult fixed-dose epinephrine without a weight-based calculation
Use the same defibrillation energy as an adult regardless of weight

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Take the leader role and assign clear roles (compressions, airway, meds, recorder)
Direct effective ventilation, since the arrest is likely respiratory
Use closed-loop communication for every order and dose
Verify weight-based doses with the length-based tape before giving them
Rotate compressors every 2 minutes and keep the family informed
HR and perfusion improve with effective ventilation and compressions
Return of spontaneous circulation; the child is transferred to the PICU
Closed-loop communication keeps the team coordinated
Weight-based dosing via the tape prevents a medication error
Compressions are frequently interrupted, lowering perfusion
A dosing error occurs because the length-based tape was skipped
No clear leader, so roles overlap and care is chaotic
Ventilation is neglected even though the arrest was respiratory

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
